topic: DLP4710 Projection Lens Selection for Marin Globe — Friday Feb 27
summary: Mirror relay lens system selected for DLP4710 Marin Globe with modular design approach for future flexibility.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. We're here to decide on the DLP4710 projection lens configuration for the Marin Globe project. Palmer's optics analysis points to three primary options: fisheye, ultra-short throw, or mirror relay systems. Key constraints are sphere coverage, positioning flexibility, and cost impact. James, what's your read on the technical requirements?

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: The DLP4710 gives us 4K resolution at 120Hz, but the lens system determines our coverage geometry. For a full sphere installation, we need minimum 180-degree field coverage. Fisheye gets us there with single projection but introduces significant distortion correction overhead. Mirror relay maintains image fidelity but requires precise mechanical alignment. What's the target sphere diameter?

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Before we get lost in technical specifications, what's the user experience we're designing for? Are people walking inside this sphere, or observing from outside? The lens choice fundamentally changes how content feels. Fisheye creates immersion but can feel claustrophobic. UST systems give you clean edges but limited sweet spots for viewing.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: I'm leaning toward the mirror relay approach. Yes, it's more complex mechanically, but it gives us the most flexibility for content rendering without software distortion compensation eating compute cycles. We can position the projector outside the sphere entirely, which simplifies cooling and maintenance access. What's the cost delta between approaches?

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: That's exactly what I was going to ask. We need to understand the full system cost, not just the lens component. Mirror relay sounds like it needs custom machining, alignment fixtures, maybe even ongoing calibration. How does that impact our unit economics if we're planning to scale this beyond Marin?

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: From a financial modeling perspective, the mirror relay has higher upfront capital requirements but potentially lower operational costs over time. The fisheye approach might have lower hardware costs but higher computational overhead, which means more powerful processing units. We need to model the total cost of ownership over a 5-year deployment cycle.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: There's a deeper question here about the psychological impact of the viewing experience. Spherical projection can create profound emotional responses - both positive transcendent experiences and negative claustrophobic reactions. The lens choice affects how people relate to the content spatially. Are we creating a cathedral-like experience or something more intimate?

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Jordan raises a good point about the experiential impact. From a content creation standpoint, fisheye projection requires all our visual assets to be rendered with that distortion baked in. That's a significant production pipeline consideration. Mirror relay keeps our content creation workflow standard, which means we can repurpose assets across different display systems.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: Alex just hit on something critical. Fisheye locks us into a single projection method. Mirror relay gives us modularity - we could theoretically use the same projector with different mirror configurations for flat screens, curved displays, whatever. That's the kind of flexibility that pays dividends long-term.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: Elon's modularity point is solid, but let's not underestimate the mechanical precision required for mirror relay systems. We're talking micron-level alignment tolerances. Any vibration, thermal expansion, or settling over time degrades the image quality. The fisheye approach is essentially set-and-forget once calibrated.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: James is right about the mechanical complexity, but I keep coming back to the user experience. A properly executed mirror relay system gives you that magical feeling where the technology disappears. Fisheye always has that slightly artificial fish-bowl distortion that reminds people they're looking at a projection. For something called the "Marin Globe," shouldn't the technology be invisible?

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Steve, that premium experience comes at a premium cost. We need to be realistic about what the market will bear. If mirror relay doubles our system cost, we're pricing ourselves out of potential installations. What's our target price point for the complete system?

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: Based on comparable immersive display installations I've analyzed, we're looking at $150K to $300K for premium planetarium-grade systems. If we can deliver mirror relay quality in the $200K range, that positions us competitively against established players while maintaining healthy margins.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: $200K is achievable with mirror relay if we design the mechanical system for manufacturability from day one. We don't need aerospace-grade precision - just automotive-grade repeatability. I'd rather spend engineering time upfront getting the mechanics right than dealing with ongoing software distortion correction overhead.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: There's also the question of long-term reliability and maintenance. Families or institutions investing in this system need confidence it will work consistently for years. Mirror systems have moving parts, alignment issues, potential points of failure. Fisheye systems are essentially solid-state once installed.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Jordan raises the service and support angle we haven't fully considered. Mirror relay means we need trained technicians for installations and maintenance. Fisheye systems could potentially be installed by AV integrators with standard projector experience. That affects our go-to-market strategy significantly.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: Let me throw out a hybrid approach: what if we design for mirror relay as the premium configuration but also support fisheye operation as a fallback? Same projector, modular lens assembly. Customers could start with fisheye and upgrade to mirror relay later.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: James, that's interesting but it sounds like we're designing two different products. Each approach optimizes for different constraints. We risk creating a system that does neither particularly well. I'd rather perfect one approach than compromise on both.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: Steve has a point about focus, but modularity doesn't have to mean compromise. If we design the projector mounting and control systems properly, the lens assembly could be completely swappable. Same electronics, same software, just different optical paths.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Let me synthesize what I'm hearing. We have three viable approaches, each with clear tradeoffs. Mirror relay gives us the best image quality and content flexibility but highest complexity and cost. Fisheye is simpler and cheaper but constrains our content pipeline and user experience. Ultra-short throw is the compromise position. Given our timeline and the fact that Marin is essentially a proof-of-concept for future installations, I'm hearing the strongest technical case for mirror relay with modular design consideration. James, can you deliver a technical specification for mirror relay system requirements? Steve, we need user experience testing criteria. Elon, I need you to validate the mechanical engineering feasibility and cost estimates.

actionItems.text: Develop mirror relay technical specification document for DLP4710 system
Define user experience testing criteria for spherical projection systems
Validate mirror relay mechanical engineering feasibility and cost model
Develop modular lens assembly design concept for future flexibility

topPriorities: Mirror relay technical specifications and mechanical design requirements
User experience testing criteria and psychological impact assessment
Cost modeling and manufacturability validation for mirror relay approach

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
